name: Louis XVI
summary_short: King of France during the early French Revolution; his loss of authority and execution became pivotal in the revolutionary crisis.

name: Maximilien Robespierre
summary_short: A leading revolutionary figure associated with the Jacobins and the radical phase of the Revolution, especially the Reign of Terror.

name: Napoleon Bonaparte
summary_short: French military and political leader who rose during the revolutionary era, seized power in 1799, and later became Emperor.

name: Otto von Bismarck
summary_short: Prussian statesman who unified Germany and helped shape great-power diplomacy during the high imperial era.

name: Cecil Rhodes
summary_short: British imperialist and mining magnate who expanded chartered-company rule in southern Africa.

name: Leopold II
summary_short: King of Belgium whose private control of the Congo Free State became central to imperial exploitation debates.

name: David Livingstone
summary_short: Scottish missionary-explorer whose travels in Africa influenced public opinion and imperial-era exploration narratives.

name: Henry Morton Stanley
summary_short: Journalist and explorer who mapped Central African routes later used in colonial expansion, especially in the Congo basin.

name: Karl Marx
summary_short: German theorist whose critique of capitalism shaped socialist and labor movements during industrialization.

name: Friedrich Engels
summary_short: German social theorist and collaborator of Marx who documented industrial working conditions and class conflict.

name: Andrew Carnegie
summary_short: Scottish-American steel industrialist who symbolized large-scale corporate growth in the Second Industrial Revolution.

name: J. P. Morgan
summary_short: American financier who consolidated major corporations and influenced industrial capital networks at global scale.

name: Thomas Edison
summary_short: American inventor whose electric lighting and power-system ventures accelerated late-industrial urban transformation.

name: Queen Victoria
summary_short: British monarch whose reign coincided with industrial expansion and the peak phase of British imperial influence.

name: Menelik II
summary_short: Emperor of Ethiopia who defeated Italy at Adwa, preserving Ethiopian sovereignty during the Scramble for Africa.

name: Marie Antoinette
summary_short: Queen of France whose political symbolism and execution became central to revolutionary politics.

name: Georges Danton
summary_short: Prominent revolutionary leader associated with mobilization in 1792 and early revolutionary government.

name: James Watt
summary_short: Scottish engineer whose steam-engine improvements greatly expanded the practical use of steam power in industry and transport.

name: George Stephenson
summary_short: Railway engineer associated with early locomotive development and the spread of rail transport in industrial Britain.

name: Henry Bessemer
summary_short: English inventor of the Bessemer steelmaking process, which sharply reduced steel costs and enabled large-scale industrial expansion.

name: Samuel Morse
summary_short: American inventor linked to practical electric telegraphy and Morse code, helping accelerate long-distance communication.

name: Richard Arkwright
summary_short: English inventor and entrepreneur whose spinning-frame and mill model helped establish factory-scale textile production.

name: James Hargreaves
summary_short: English weaver and inventor of the spinning jenny, an early catalyst of textile mechanization.

name: Eli Whitney
summary_short: American inventor of the cotton gin, which accelerated cotton processing and textile supply chains.

name: Richard Trevithick
summary_short: Cornish engineer who pioneered high-pressure steam engines and early locomotive applications.

name: Isambard Kingdom Brunel
summary_short: British engineer associated with major railway, bridge, and steamship projects in the transport revolution.

name: Robert Owen
summary_short: Welsh social reformer and mill manager who advocated labor protections and cooperative social models.

name: Lord Shaftesbury
summary_short: British reform politician associated with campaigns for factory and child-labor protections.

name: Karl Benz
summary_short: German engineer associated with practical internal-combustion automotive design in the late 19th century.

name: Nikola Tesla
summary_short: Inventor and electrical engineer linked to alternating-current systems that expanded practical electric-power distribution.

name: Henry Ford
summary_short: American industrialist associated with scalable assembly-line manufacturing and modern mass production.

name: Annie Besant
summary_short: British activist who supported the 1888 matchgirls strike and broader labor and social reform campaigns.

name: Franz Ferdinand
summary_short: Heir to the Austro-Hungarian throne whose assassination in Sarajevo triggered the July Crisis.

name: Gavrilo Princip
summary_short: Bosnian Serb nationalist who assassinated Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo in June 1914.

name: Wilhelm II
summary_short: German emperor during the prewar arms race and most of World War I.

name: Nicholas II
summary_short: Last tsar of Russia, overthrown in 1917 amid wartime crisis and domestic unrest.

name: Woodrow Wilson
summary_short: U.S. president during American entry into World War I and a leading figure at Paris in 1919.

name: Vladimir Lenin
summary_short: Bolshevik leader who helped pull Russia out of World War I and reshape European politics.

name: David Lloyd George
summary_short: British prime minister for most of the war who pushed total mobilization and postwar settlement goals.

name: Georges Clemenceau
summary_short: French wartime leader known for demanding a hard peace settlement after major wartime losses.

name: Ferdinand Foch
summary_short: French marshal who coordinated Allied command in 1918 during the final offensives.

name: Douglas Haig
summary_short: Senior British commander on the Western Front during major attritional battles.

name: John J. Pershing
summary_short: Commander of the American Expeditionary Forces after U.S. entry into the war.

name: Philippe Pétain
summary_short: French general associated with the defense of Verdun during one of the war's bloodiest campaigns.

name: Erich Ludendorff
summary_short: German general who helped shape late-war strategy, including the 1918 spring offensives.

name: Paul von Hindenburg
summary_short: German field marshal celebrated after Tannenberg and later central to wartime command.

name: Helmuth von Moltke the Younger
summary_short: German chief of staff at the start of the war, associated with early war-planning execution.

name: Theobald von Bethmann Hollweg
summary_short: German chancellor during escalation and early war years, involved in July Crisis decision-making.

name: Franz Joseph I
summary_short: Emperor of Austria-Hungary whose state issued the ultimatum to Serbia in 1914.

name: Mehmed V
summary_short: Ottoman sultan during the empire's wartime alignment with the Central Powers.

name: Enver Pasha
summary_short: Ottoman leader tied to wartime strategy and coercive internal wartime policies.

name: Mustafa Kemal Atatürk
summary_short: Ottoman officer whose role at Gallipoli raised his profile before the Turkish national movement.

name: T. E. Lawrence
summary_short: British liaison figure associated with the Arab Revolt against Ottoman rule.

name: Hussein bin Ali
summary_short: Sharif of Mecca who launched the Arab Revolt during the war.

name: Nikolai Brusilov
summary_short: Russian general whose 1916 offensive temporarily pressured Austro-Hungarian forces.

name: Alexander Kerensky
summary_short: Russian Provisional Government leader between the February and Bolshevik revolutions.

name: Winston Churchill
summary_short: British statesman involved in wartime strategy debates, especially around Gallipoli and mobilization.

name: Vittorio Orlando
summary_short: Italian prime minister at the Paris Peace Conference representing Italian territorial claims.

name: Alfred von Tirpitz
summary_short: German naval strategist linked to prewar and wartime naval expansion policies.
